summary:
Megiddo was one of the most strategically important Canaanite city-states of the Bronze Age, located at the head of a pass through the Carmel mountain range that controlled the main route connecting Egypt with Mesopotamia and Anatolia via the Jezreel Valley. Its commanding position made it a key prize for imperial powers and a site of repeated military conflict over millennia. During the Bronze Age, roughly 2000–1100 BC, Megiddo was a wealthy, well-fortified city with substantial palace and temple complexes, extensive trade connections, and an elite whose material culture shows strong Egyptian, Canaanite, and broader Near Eastern influences.

The city's importance is illustrated by the [[event:1803|Battle of Megiddo]] around 1457 BC, in which the Egyptian pharaoh [[event:1802|Thutmose III]] defeated a coalition of Canaanite rulers, establishing firm Egyptian control over the region and demonstrating Megiddo's role as the focal point for resistance to Egyptian imperial expansion into Canaan. Later, Megiddo appears in the [[event:1881|Amarna Letters]] as one of the cities writing to the Egyptian court seeking military support.

Megiddo accumulated an enormous tell — a mound created by successive layers of habitation — through approximately twenty distinct occupation phases. Excavations beginning in the early twentieth century revealed this remarkable sequence of cities. The site is also biblically significant: Megiddo appears in the Hebrew Bible as a site of important battles and in the New Testament book of Revelation as Armageddon (from Hebrew Har Megiddo, Mountain of Megiddo), the prophesied site of the final battle at the end of time.
